name: keyboard

metadata.name: keyboard
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: Hook and simulate keyboard events on Windows and Linux

documentation:
keyboard
========

Take full control of your keyboard with this small Python library. Hook global events, register hotkeys, simulate key presses and much more.

## Features

- **Global event hook** on all keyboards (captures keys regardless of focus).
- **Listen** and **send** keyboard events.
- Works with **Windows** and **Linux** (requires sudo), with experimental **OS X** support (thanks @glitchassassin!).
- **Pure Python**, no C modules to be compiled.
- **Zero dependencies**. Trivial to install and deploy, just copy the files.
- **Python 2 and 3**.
- Complex hotkey support (e.g. `ctrl+shift+m, ctrl+space`) with controllable timeout.
- Includes **high level API** (e.g. [record](#keyboard.record) and [play](#keyboard.play), [add_abbreviation](#keyboard.add_abbreviation)).
- Maps keys as they actually are in your layout, with **full internationalization support** (e.g. `Ctrl+ç`).
- Events automatically captured in separate thread, doesn't block main program.
- Tested and documented.
- Doesn't break accented dead keys (I'm looking at you, pyHook).
- Mouse support available via project [mouse](https://github.com/boppreh/mouse) (`pip install mouse`).

## Usage

Install the [PyPI package](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/keyboard/):

    pip install keyboard

or clone the repository (no installation required, source files are sufficient):

    git clone https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard

or [download and extract the zip](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/archive/master.zip) into your project folder.

Then check the [API docs below](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard#api) to see what features are available.


## Example


```py
import keyboard

keyboard.press_and_release('shift+s, space')

keyboard.write('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.')

keyboard.add_hotkey('ctrl+shift+a', print, args=('triggered', 'hotkey'))

# Press PAGE UP then PAGE DOWN to type "foobar".
keyboard.add_hotkey('page up, page down', lambda: keyboard.write('foobar'))

# Blocks until you press esc.
keyboard.wait('esc')

# Record events until 'esc' is pressed.
recorded = keyboard.record(until='esc')
# Then replay back at three times the speed.
keyboard.play(recorded, speed_factor=3)

# Type @@ then press space to replace with abbreviation.
keyboard.add_abbreviation('@@', 'my.long.email@example.com')

# Block forever, like `while True`.
keyboard.wait()
```

## Known limitations:

- Events generated under Windows don't report device id (`event.device == None`). [#21](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/21)
- Media keys on Linux may appear nameless (scan-code only) or not at all. [#20](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/20)
- Key suppression/blocking only available on Windows. [#22](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/22)
- To avoid depending on X, the Linux parts reads raw device files (`/dev/input/input*`)
but this requires root.
- Other applications, such as some games, may register hooks that swallow all 
key events. In this case `keyboard` will be unable to report events.
- This program makes no attempt to hide itself, so don't use it for keyloggers or online gaming bots. Be responsible.

modules.name: keyboard._generic
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

methods.name: keyboard._generic.GenericListener.start_if_necessary
documentation: Starts the listening thread if it wasn't already.

methods.name: keyboard._generic.GenericListener.process
documentation: Loops over the underlying queue of events and processes them in order.

methods.name: keyboard._generic.GenericListener.add_handler
documentation:
Adds a function to receive each event captured, starting the capturing
process if necessary.

methods.name: keyboard._generic.GenericListener.remove_handler
documentation: Removes a previously added event handler.

modules.name: keyboard.mouse
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.is_pressed
documentation: Returns True if the given button is currently pressed.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.press
documentation: Presses the given button (but doesn't release).

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.release
documentation: Releases the given button.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.click
documentation: Sends a click with the given button.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.double_click
documentation: Sends a double click with the given button.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.right_click
documentation: Sends a right click with the given button.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.wheel
documentation: Scrolls the wheel `delta` clicks. Sign indicates direction.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.move
documentation:
Moves the mouse. If `absolute`, to position (x, y), otherwise move relative
to the current position. If `duration` is non-zero, animates the movement.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.drag
documentation:
Holds the left mouse button, moving from start to end position, then
releases. `absolute` and `duration` are parameters regarding the mouse
movement.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.on_button
documentation: Invokes `callback` with `args` when the specified event happens.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.on_click
documentation: Invokes `callback` with `args` when the left button is clicked.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.on_double_click
documentation: Invokes `callback` with `args` when the left button is double clicked.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.on_right_click
documentation: Invokes `callback` with `args` when the right button is clicked.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.on_middle_click
documentation: Invokes `callback` with `args` when the middle button is clicked.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.wait
documentation: Blocks program execution until the given button performs an event.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.get_position
documentation: Returns the (x, y) mouse position.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.hook
documentation:
Installs a global listener on all available mouses, invoking `callback`
each time it is moved, a key status changes or the wheel is spun. A mouse
event is passed as argument, with type either `mouse.ButtonEvent`,
`mouse.WheelEvent` or `mouse.MoveEvent`.

Returns the given callback for easier development.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.unhook
documentation: Removes a previously installed hook.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.unhook_all
documentation:
Removes all hooks registered by this application. Note this may include
hooks installed by high level functions, such as `record`.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.record
documentation:
Records all mouse events until the user presses the given button.
Then returns the list of events recorded. Pairs well with `play(events)`.

Note: this is a blocking function.
Note: for more details on the mouse hook and events see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.mouse.play
documentation:
Plays a sequence of recorded events, maintaining the relative time
intervals. If speed_factor is <= 0 then the actions are replayed as fast
as the OS allows. Pairs well with `record()`.

The parameters `include_*` define if events of that type should be inluded
in the replay or ignored.

functions.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.press
documentation: Sends a 'down' event for the specified scan code

functions.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.release
documentation: Sends an 'up' event for the specified scan code

functions.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.map_name
documentation:
Returns a tuple of (scan_code, modifiers) where `scan_code` is a numeric scan code
and `modifiers` is an array of string modifier names (like 'shift')

functions.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.name_from_scancode
documentation: Returns the name or character associated with the specified key code

variables.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.key_controller
documentation: Exported functions below

methods.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.KeyMap.character_to_vk
documentation:
Returns a tuple of (scan_code, modifiers) where `scan_code` is a numeric scan code
and `modifiers` is an array of string modifier names (like 'shift')

methods.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.KeyMap.vk_to_character
documentation:
Returns a character corresponding to the specified scan code (with given
modifiers applied)

methods.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.KeyController.press
documentation: Sends a 'down' event for the specified scan code

methods.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.KeyController.release
documentation: Sends an 'up' event for the specified scan code

methods.name: keyboard._darwinkeyboard.KeyEventListener.run
documentation:
Creates a listener and loops while waiting for an event. Intended to run as
a background thread.

modules.name: keyboard._nixmouse
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: keyboard._winkeyboard
documentation:
This is the Windows backend for keyboard events, and is implemented by
invoking the Win32 API through the ctypes module. This is error prone
and can introduce very unpythonic failure modes, such as segfaults and
low level memory leaks. But it is also dependency-free, very performant
well documented on Microsoft's website and scattered examples.

# TODO:
- Keypad numbers still print as numbers even when numlock is off.
- No way to specify if user wants a keypad key or not in `map_char`.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard._winkeyboard._setup_name_tables
documentation:
Ensures the scan code/virtual key code/name translation tables are
filled.

functions.name: keyboard._winkeyboard.prepare_intercept
documentation:
Registers a Windows low level keyboard hook. The provided callback will
be invoked for each high-level keyboard event, and is expected to return
True if the key event should be passed to the next program, or False if
the event is to be blocked.

No event is processed until the Windows messages are pumped (see
start_intercept).

variables.name: keyboard._winkeyboard.WM_SYSKEYDOWN
documentation: Used for ALT key

classes.name: keyboard._winkeyboard.MOUSEINPUT
documentation: Included for completeness.

modules.name: keyboard._winmouse
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: keyboard._mouse_event
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

modules.name: keyboard._keyboard_tests
documentation:
Side effects are avoided using two techniques:

- Low level OS requests (keyboard._os_keyboard) are mocked out by rewriting
the functions at that namespace. This includes a list of dummy keys.
- Events are pumped manually by the main test class, and accepted events
are tested against expected values.

Fake user events are appended to `input_events`, passed through
keyboard,_listener.direct_callback, then, if accepted, appended to
`output_events`. Fake OS events (keyboard.press) are processed
and added to `output_events` immediately, mimicking real functionality.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

methods.name: keyboard._keyboard_tests.TestKeyboard.test_write_stash_restore
documentation: restore_state_after has been removed after the introduction of `restore_modifiers`.
def test_write_stash_not_restore(self):
self.do(d_shift)
keyboard.write('a', restore_state_after=False, exact=False)
self.do([], u_shift+d_a+u_a)

modules.name: keyboard._nixkeyboard
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard._nixkeyboard.cleanup_key
documentation: Formats a dumpkeys format to our standard.
TODO: start by reading current keyboard state, as to not missing any already pressed keys.
See: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3649874/how-to-get-keyboard-state-in-linux

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.init
documentation: Initializes mouse state
Exports

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.listen
documentation: Appends events to the queue (ButtonEvent, WheelEvent, and MoveEvent).

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.press
documentation: Sends a down event for the specified button, using the provided constants

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.release
documentation: Sends an up event for the specified button, using the provided constants

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.wheel
documentation:
Sends a wheel event for the provided number of clicks. May be negative to reverse
direction.

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.move_to
documentation: Sets the mouse's location to the specified coordinates.

functions.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.get_position
documentation: Returns the mouse's location as a tuple of (x, y).

methods.name: keyboard._darwinmouse.MouseEventListener.run
documentation:
Creates a listener and loops while waiting for an event. Intended to run as
a background thread.

modules.name: keyboard._canonical_names
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard._canonical_names.normalize_name
documentation:
Given a key name (e.g. "LEFT CONTROL"), clean up the string and convert to
the canonical representation (e.g. "left ctrl") if one is known.

modules.name: keyboard
documentation:
keyboard
========

Take full control of your keyboard with this small Python library. Hook global events, register hotkeys, simulate key presses and much more.

## Features

- **Global event hook** on all keyboards (captures keys regardless of focus).
- **Listen** and **send** keyboard events.
- Works with **Windows** and **Linux** (requires sudo), with experimental **OS X** support (thanks @glitchassassin!).
- **Pure Python**, no C modules to be compiled.
- **Zero dependencies**. Trivial to install and deploy, just copy the files.
- **Python 2 and 3**.
- Complex hotkey support (e.g. `ctrl+shift+m, ctrl+space`) with controllable timeout.
- Includes **high level API** (e.g. [record](#keyboard.record) and [play](#keyboard.play), [add_abbreviation](#keyboard.add_abbreviation)).
- Maps keys as they actually are in your layout, with **full internationalization support** (e.g. `Ctrl+ç`).
- Events automatically captured in separate thread, doesn't block main program.
- Tested and documented.
- Doesn't break accented dead keys (I'm looking at you, pyHook).
- Mouse support available via project [mouse](https://github.com/boppreh/mouse) (`pip install mouse`).

## Usage

Install the [PyPI package](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/keyboard/):

    pip install keyboard

or clone the repository (no installation required, source files are sufficient):

    git clone https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard

or [download and extract the zip](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/archive/master.zip) into your project folder.

Then check the [API docs below](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard#api) to see what features are available.


## Example


```py
import keyboard

keyboard.press_and_release('shift+s, space')

keyboard.write('The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.')

keyboard.add_hotkey('ctrl+shift+a', print, args=('triggered', 'hotkey'))

# Press PAGE UP then PAGE DOWN to type "foobar".
keyboard.add_hotkey('page up, page down', lambda: keyboard.write('foobar'))

# Blocks until you press esc.
keyboard.wait('esc')

# Record events until 'esc' is pressed.
recorded = keyboard.record(until='esc')
# Then replay back at three times the speed.
keyboard.play(recorded, speed_factor=3)

# Type @@ then press space to replace with abbreviation.
keyboard.add_abbreviation('@@', 'my.long.email@example.com')

# Block forever, like `while True`.
keyboard.wait()
```

## Known limitations:

- Events generated under Windows don't report device id (`event.device == None`). [#21](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/21)
- Media keys on Linux may appear nameless (scan-code only) or not at all. [#20](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/20)
- Key suppression/blocking only available on Windows. [#22](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/issues/22)
- To avoid depending on X, the Linux parts reads raw device files (`/dev/input/input*`)
but this requires root.
- Other applications, such as some games, may register hooks that swallow all 
key events. In this case `keyboard` will be unable to report events.
- This program makes no attempt to hide itself, so don't use it for keyloggers or online gaming bots. Be responsible.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: keyboard.is_modifier
documentation: Returns True if `key` is a scan code or name of a modifier key.

functions.name: keyboard.key_to_scan_codes
documentation: Returns a list of scan codes associated with this key (name or scan code).

functions.name: keyboard.parse_hotkey
documentation:
Parses a user-provided hotkey into nested tuples representing the
parsed structure, with the bottom values being lists of scan codes.
Also accepts raw scan codes, which are then wrapped in the required
number of nestings.

Example:

    parse_hotkey("alt+shift+a, alt+b, c")
    #    Keys:    ^~^ ^~~~^ ^  ^~^ ^  ^
    #    Steps:   ^~~~~~~~~~^  ^~~~^  ^

    # ((alt_codes, shift_codes, a_codes), (alt_codes, b_codes), (c_codes,))

functions.name: keyboard.send
documentation:
Sends OS events that perform the given *hotkey* hotkey.

- `hotkey` can be either a scan code (e.g. 57 for space), single key
(e.g. 'space') or multi-key, multi-step hotkey (e.g. 'alt+F4, enter').
- `do_press` if true then press events are sent. Defaults to True.
- `do_release` if true then release events are sent. Defaults to True.

    send(57)
    send('ctrl+alt+del')
    send('alt+F4, enter')
    send('shift+s')

Note: keys are released in the opposite order they were pressed.

functions.name: keyboard.press
documentation: Presses and holds down a hotkey (see `send`).

functions.name: keyboard.release
documentation: Releases a hotkey (see `send`).

functions.name: keyboard.is_pressed
documentation:
Returns True if the key is pressed.

    is_pressed(57) #-> True
    is_pressed('space') #-> True
    is_pressed('ctrl+space') #-> True

functions.name: keyboard.call_later
documentation:
Calls the provided function in a new thread after waiting some time.
Useful for giving the system some time to process an event, without blocking
the current execution flow.

functions.name: keyboard.hook
documentation:
Installs a global listener on all available keyboards, invoking `callback`
each time a key is pressed or released.

The event passed to the callback is of type `keyboard.KeyboardEvent`,
with the following attributes:

- `name`: an Unicode representation of the character (e.g. "&") or
description (e.g.  "space"). The name is always lower-case.
- `scan_code`: number representing the physical key, e.g. 55.
- `time`: timestamp of the time the event occurred, with as much precision
as given by the OS.

Returns the given callback for easier development.

functions.name: keyboard.on_press
documentation: Invokes `callback` for every KEY_DOWN event. For details see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.on_release
documentation: Invokes `callback` for every KEY_UP event. For details see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.hook_key
documentation:
Hooks key up and key down events for a single key. Returns the event handler
created. To remove a hooked key use `unhook_key(key)` or
`unhook_key(handler)`.

Note: this function shares state with hotkeys, so `clear_all_hotkeys`
affects it as well.

functions.name: keyboard.on_press_key
documentation: Invokes `callback` for KEY_DOWN event related to the given key. For details see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.on_release_key
documentation: Invokes `callback` for KEY_UP event related to the given key. For details see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.unhook
documentation:
Removes a previously added hook, either by callback or by the return value
of `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.unhook_all
documentation:
Removes all keyboard hooks in use, including hotkeys, abbreviations, word
listeners, `record`ers and `wait`s.

functions.name: keyboard.block_key
documentation: Suppresses all key events of the given key, regardless of modifiers.

functions.name: keyboard.remap_key
documentation:
Whenever the key `src` is pressed or released, regardless of modifiers,
press or release the hotkey `dst` instead.

functions.name: keyboard.parse_hotkey_combinations
documentation:
Parses a user-provided hotkey. Differently from `parse_hotkey`,
instead of each step being a list of the different scan codes for each key,
each step is a list of all possible combinations of those scan codes.

functions.name: keyboard._add_hotkey_step
documentation: Hooks a single-step hotkey (e.g. 'shift+a').

functions.name: keyboard.add_hotkey
documentation:
Invokes a callback every time a hotkey is pressed. The hotkey must
be in the format `ctrl+shift+a, s`. This would trigger when the user holds
ctrl, shift and "a" at once, releases, and then presses "s". To represent
literal commas, pluses, and spaces, use their names ('comma', 'plus',
'space').

- `args` is an optional list of arguments to passed to the callback during
each invocation.
- `suppress` defines if successful triggers should block the keys from being
sent to other programs.
- `timeout` is the amount of seconds allowed to pass between key presses.
- `trigger_on_release` if true, the callback is invoked on key release instead
of key press.

The event handler function is returned. To remove a hotkey call
`remove_hotkey(hotkey)` or `remove_hotkey(handler)`.
before the hotkey state is reset.

Note: hotkeys are activated when the last key is *pressed*, not released.
Note: the callback is executed in a separate thread, asynchronously. For an
example of how to use a callback synchronously, see `wait`.

Examples:

    # Different but equivalent ways to listen for a spacebar key press.
    add_hotkey(' ', print, args=['space was pressed'])
    add_hotkey('space', print, args=['space was pressed'])
    add_hotkey('Space', print, args=['space was pressed'])
    # Here 57 represents the keyboard code for spacebar; so you will be
    # pressing 'spacebar', not '57' to activate the print function.
    add_hotkey(57, print, args=['space was pressed'])

    add_hotkey('ctrl+q', quit)
    add_hotkey('ctrl+alt+enter, space', some_callback)

functions.name: keyboard.remove_hotkey
documentation:
Removes a previously hooked hotkey. Must be called with the value returned
by `add_hotkey`.

functions.name: keyboard.unhook_all_hotkeys
documentation:
Removes all keyboard hotkeys in use, including abbreviations, word listeners,
`record`ers and `wait`s.

functions.name: keyboard.remap_hotkey
documentation:
Whenever the hotkey `src` is pressed, suppress it and send
`dst` instead.

Example:

    remap('alt+w', 'ctrl+up')

functions.name: keyboard.stash_state
documentation:
Builds a list of all currently pressed scan codes, releases them and returns
the list. Pairs well with `restore_state` and `restore_modifiers`.

functions.name: keyboard.restore_state
documentation:
Given a list of scan_codes ensures these keys, and only these keys, are
pressed. Pairs well with `stash_state`, alternative to `restore_modifiers`.

functions.name: keyboard.restore_modifiers
documentation: Like `restore_state`, but only restores modifier keys.

functions.name: keyboard.write
documentation:
Sends artificial keyboard events to the OS, simulating the typing of a given
text. Characters not available on the keyboard are typed as explicit unicode
characters using OS-specific functionality, such as alt+codepoint.

To ensure text integrity, all currently pressed keys are released before
the text is typed, and modifiers are restored afterwards.

- `delay` is the number of seconds to wait between keypresses, defaults to
no delay.
- `restore_state_after` can be used to restore the state of pressed keys
after the text is typed, i.e. presses the keys that were released at the
beginning. Defaults to True.
- `exact` forces typing all characters as explicit unicode (e.g.
alt+codepoint or special events). If None, uses platform-specific suggested
value.

functions.name: keyboard.wait
documentation:
Blocks the program execution until the given hotkey is pressed or,
if given no parameters, blocks forever.

functions.name: keyboard.get_hotkey_name
keyboard.read_event
documentation: Blocks until a keyboard event happens, then returns that event.

functions.name: keyboard.read_key
documentation:
Blocks until a keyboard event happens, then returns that event's name or,
if missing, its scan code.

functions.name: keyboard.read_hotkey
documentation:
Similar to `read_key()`, but blocks until the user presses and releases a
hotkey (or single key), then returns a string representing the hotkey
pressed.

Example:

    read_hotkey()
    # "ctrl+shift+p"

functions.name: keyboard.get_typed_strings
documentation:
Given a sequence of events, tries to deduce what strings were typed.
Strings are separated when a non-textual key is pressed (such as tab or
enter). Characters are converted to uppercase according to shift and
capslock status. If `allow_backspace` is True, backspaces remove the last
character typed.

This function is a generator, so you can pass an infinite stream of events
and convert them to strings in real time.

Note this functions is merely an heuristic. Windows for example keeps per-
process keyboard state such as keyboard layout, and this information is not
available for our hooks.

    get_type_strings(record()) #-> ['This is what', 'I recorded', '']

functions.name: keyboard.start_recording
documentation:
Starts recording all keyboard events into a global variable, or the given
queue if any. Returns the queue of events and the hooked function.

Use `stop_recording()` or `unhook(hooked_function)` to stop.

functions.name: keyboard.stop_recording
documentation:
Stops the global recording of events and returns a list of the events
captured.

functions.name: keyboard.record
documentation:
Records all keyboard events from all keyboards until the user presses the
given hotkey. Then returns the list of events recorded, of type
`keyboard.KeyboardEvent`. Pairs well with
`play(events)`.

Note: this is a blocking function.
Note: for more details on the keyboard hook and events see `hook`.

functions.name: keyboard.play
documentation:
Plays a sequence of recorded events, maintaining the relative time
intervals. If speed_factor is <= 0 then the actions are replayed as fast
as the OS allows. Pairs well with `record()`.

Note: the current keyboard state is cleared at the beginning and restored at
the end of the function.

functions.name: keyboard.add_word_listener
documentation:
Invokes a callback every time a sequence of characters is typed (e.g. 'pet')
and followed by a trigger key (e.g. space). Modifiers (e.g. alt, ctrl,
shift) are ignored.

- `word` the typed text to be matched. E.g. 'pet'.
- `callback` is an argument-less function to be invoked each time the word
is typed.
- `triggers` is the list of keys that will cause a match to be checked. If
the user presses some key that is not a character (len>1) and not in
triggers, the characters so far will be discarded. By default the trigger
is only `space`.
- `match_suffix` defines if endings of words should also be checked instead
of only whole words. E.g. if true, typing 'carpet'+space will trigger the
listener for 'pet'. Defaults to false, only whole words are checked.
- `timeout` is the maximum number of seconds between typed characters before
the current word is discarded. Defaults to 2 seconds.

Returns the event handler created. To remove a word listener use
`remove_word_listener(word)` or `remove_word_listener(handler)`.

Note: all actions are performed on key down. Key up events are ignored.
Note: word matches are **case sensitive**.

functions.name: keyboard.remove_word_listener
documentation:
Removes a previously registered word listener. Accepts either the word used
during registration (exact string) or the event handler returned by the
`add_word_listener` or `add_abbreviation` functions.

functions.name: keyboard.add_abbreviation
documentation:
Registers a hotkey that replaces one typed text with another. For example

    add_abbreviation('tm', u'™')

Replaces every "tm" followed by a space with a ™ symbol (and no space). The
replacement is done by sending backspace events.

- `match_suffix` defines if endings of words should also be checked instead
of only whole words. E.g. if true, typing 'carpet'+space will trigger the
listener for 'pet'. Defaults to false, only whole words are checked.
- `timeout` is the maximum number of seconds between typed characters before
the current word is discarded. Defaults to 2 seconds.

For more details see `add_word_listener`.

classes.name: keyboard._State
documentation: Just a dynamic object to store attributes for the closures.

classes.name: keyboard._Event
documentation: The "Event" class from `threading` ignores signals when waiting and is
impossible to interrupt with Ctrl+C. So we rewrite `wait` to wait in small,
interruptible intervals.

methods.name: keyboard._KeyboardListener.direct_callback
documentation:
This function is called for every OS keyboard event and decides if the
event should be blocked or not, and passes a copy of the event to
other, non-blocking, listeners.

There are two ways to block events: remapped keys, which translate
events by suppressing and re-emitting; and blocked hotkeys, which
suppress specific hotkeys.

modules.name: keyboard._mouse_tests
